quotes.text: It's very hard in the beginning to understand that the whole idea is not to beat the other runners. Eventually you learn that the competition is against the little voice inside you.
quotes.author: George Sheehan

quotes.text: Pain is inevitable. Suffering is optional.
quotes.author: Haruki Murakami

quotes.text: The miracle isn't that I finished. The miracle is that I had the courage to start.
quotes.author: John Bingham

quotes.text: Run when you can, walk if you have to, crawl if you must; just never give up.
quotes.author: Dean Karnazes

quotes.text: If you want to become the best runner you can be, start now. Don't spend the rest of your life wondering if you can do it.
quotes.author: Priscilla Welch

quotes.text: I always loved running. It was something you could do by yourself and under your own power.
quotes.author: Jesse Owens

quotes.text: Champions keep playing until they get it right.
quotes.author: Billie Jean King

quotes.text: You have to expect things of yourself before you can do them.
quotes.author: Michael Jordan

quotes.text: It's not the will to win that matters, everyone has that. It's the will to prepare to win that matters.
quotes.author: Paul Bryant

quotes.text: The body achieves what the mind believes.
quotes.author: Napoleon Hill

quotes.text: Somewhere in the world someone is training when you are not. When you race him, he will win.
quotes.author: Tom Fleming

quotes.text: If you don't have answers to your problems after a four-hour run, you ain't getting them.
quotes.author: Christopher McDougall

quotes.text: The will to win means nothing without the will to prepare.
quotes.author: Juma Ikangaa

quotes.text: Ask yourself: 'Can I give more?' The answer is usually: 'Yes.'
quotes.author: Paul Tergat

quotes.text: To give anything less than your best is to sacrifice the gift.
quotes.author: Steve Prefontaine

quotes.text: A lot of people run a race to see who's the fastest. I run to see who has the most guts.
quotes.author: Steve Prefontaine

quotes.text: Make sure your worst enemy doesn't live between your own two ears.
quotes.author: Laird Hamilton

quotes.text: Once you're beat mentally, you might as well not even go to the starting line.
quotes.author: Todd Williams

quotes.text: Our greatest glory is not in never falling, but in rising every time we fall.
quotes.author: Confucius

quotes.text: It does not matter how slowly you go as long as you do not stop.
quotes.author: Confucius

quotes.text: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
quotes.author: Will Durant

quotes.text: Whether you think you can, or you think you can't, you're right.
quotes.author: Henry Ford

quotes.text: Continuous effort, not strength or intelligence, is the key to unlocking our potential.
quotes.author: Winston Churchill

quotes.text: It always seems impossible until it's done.
quotes.author: Nelson Mandela

quotes.text: Fall seven times, stand up eight.
quotes.author: Japanese Proverb

quotes.text: The journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step.
quotes.author: Lao Tzu

quotes.text: Hard work beats talent when talent doesn't work hard.
quotes.author: Tim Notke

quotes.text: You miss 100% of the shots you don't take.
quotes.author: Wayne Gretzky

quotes.text: I've failed over and over and over again in my life and that is why I succeed.
quotes.author: Michael Jordan

quotes.text: It is not the mountain we conquer but ourselves.
quotes.author: Edmund Hillary

quotes.text: There is no such thing as bad weather, only inappropriate clothing.
quotes.author: Alfred Wainwright

quotes.text: The man who moves a mountain begins by carrying away small stones.
quotes.author: Confucius

quotes.text: Persistence can change failure into extraordinary achievement.
quotes.author: Matt Biondi

quotes.text: If you fail to prepare, you're prepared to fail.
quotes.author: Mark Spitz

quotes.text: You're not going to determine my success. I'm going to determine my success.
quotes.author: Allyson Felix

quotes.text: Don't be afraid to fail. Be afraid not to try.
quotes.author: Michael Jordan

quotes.text: The harder the battle, the sweeter the victory.
quotes.author: Les Brown

quotes.text: Winning takes care of everything.
quotes.author: Deion Sanders

quotes.text: Some people want it to happen, some wish it would happen, others make it happen.
quotes.author: Michael Jordan

quotes.text: Today I will do what others won't, so tomorrow I can accomplish what others can't.
quotes.author: Jerry Rice

quotes.text: The only place where success comes before work is in the dictionary.
quotes.author: Vidal Sassoon

quotes.text: Strength does not come from winning. Your struggles develop your strengths.
quotes.author: Arnold Schwarzenegger

quotes.text: If you're going through hell, keep going.
quotes.author: Winston Churchill

quotes.text: You can't put a limit on anything. The more you dream, the farther you get.
quotes.author: Michael Phelps

quotes.text: There will be obstacles. There will be doubters. There will be mistakes. But with hard work, there are no limits.
quotes.author: Michael Phelps

quotes.text: I never thought about losing, but now that it's happened, the only thing is to do it right.
quotes.author: Muhammad Ali

quotes.text: I hated every minute of training, but I said, 'Don't quit. Suffer now and live the rest of your life as a champion.'
quotes.author: Muhammad Ali

quotes.text: Float like a butterfly, sting like a bee.
quotes.author: Muhammad Ali

quotes.text: He who is not courageous enough to take risks will accomplish nothing in life.
quotes.author: Muhammad Ali

quotes.text: Most people never run far enough on their first wind to find out they've got a second.
quotes.author: William James

quotes.text: Don't watch the clock; do what it does. Keep going.
quotes.author: Sam Levenson

quotes.text: A river cuts through rock, not because of its power, but because of its persistence.
quotes.author: James N. Watkins

quotes.text: Energy and persistence conquer all things.
quotes.author: Benjamin Franklin

quotes.text: What we fear doing most is usually what we most need to do.
quotes.author: Ralph Waldo Emerson

quotes.text: The race is not always to the swift, but to those who keep on running.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Nothing will work unless you do.
quotes.author: Maya Angelou

quotes.text: Do not wait to strike till the iron is hot, but make it hot by striking.
quotes.author: William Butler Yeats

quotes.text: Discipline is the bridge between goals and accomplishment.
quotes.author: Jim Rohn

quotes.text: Motivation gets you going, but discipline keeps you growing.
quotes.author: John C. Maxwell

quotes.text: Success is no accident. It is hard work, perseverance, learning, sacrifice and love of what you are doing.
quotes.author: Pele

quotes.text: The swim won't drown your doubt, but the next stroke might.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Set the alarm. The lake doesn't care that you're tired.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Three sports, one stubborn heart.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: You don't find your limit on race day. You build past it on a Tuesday.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Transition is where races are quietly won.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Pace the start, attack the finish, respect the middle.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The bike rewards patience. The run punishes ego.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Wake before the sun and the day owes you nothing.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Consistency is just talent that showed up again.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Some days you swim. Some days you survive. Both count.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Train the boring miles; race day handles the glory.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Your legs will quit a hundred times before your finish line does.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Cold water, warm resolve.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The wall is just your old self asking you to stop.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Hills don't get easier. You get harder.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Every brick you lay in winter, you cross in summer.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Discipline tastes like a 5am alarm and feels like a finish line.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Show up tired, leave proud.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The clock is honest. So train like it.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: You can't sprint a marathon, but you can refuse to walk.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Slow is smooth. Smooth is fast. Fast is earned.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Goggles fog, minds clear.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The taper is faith made physical.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Race the course you trained for, not the one you feared.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Negative splits, positive soul.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: One more lap is rarely a mistake.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The chain only moves when you push it. So do you.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Wetsuit on, excuses off.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Comfort never crossed a finish line.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Run the mile you're in.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Your watch counts seconds; your grit counts.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The road is long because the version of you at the end is worth it.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Rain is just applause from the sky for showing up.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Lactate is the language of trying.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Tri once, change forever.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Rest is a rep. Take it seriously.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The pool sees you when no one else does.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Pedal through the doubt; coast on the proof.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Mile twenty is where promises meet practice.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: You signed up scared. Finish brave.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The body keeps the receipts of every workout you skipped.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Aero is a state of mind before it's a position.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Breathe bilateral, think unilateral: forward.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: There is no off-season for who you're becoming.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The split that hurts is the split that counts.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Climb seated, summit standing.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: You can rest at the finish line. Not the start of mile two.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: A flat tire is a story. A DNF from your couch isn't.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Train like the weather can't vote.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Every great run was once a hard first step.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Your heart rate is loud because you're alive and pushing. Good.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The fastest swimmers fight the water least.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Headwinds build the legs that earn the tailwinds.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Doubt is a hill. Crest it.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Quitting is loud at mile three and silent at the finish.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: You don't need motivation today. You need your shoes on.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Brick workouts build athletes; soft beds build regret.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Swim your line. Hold your line. Cross your line.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The hardest interval is the one between the alarm and your feet.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Race smart, suffer well, smile last.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Gravity is undefeated. Climb anyway.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Cadence is calm wearing running shoes.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Empty the tank; the medal weighs nothing without it.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The finish line forgets your excuses but remembers your effort.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Open water, open heart, closed door to quitting.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Long course, short patience, infinite stubbornness.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Your future self is watching this rep.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Endurance is just patience wearing a race number.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Sweat is the ink; the finish line is the signature.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Hard now, proud forever.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The river bends, the road climbs, the runner keeps going.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: You are one workout away from a better day, always.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Chase the sunrise; the medals follow.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Strong legs whisper; strong minds finish.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Pain fades. Splits last. Pride outlives both.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: The course is honest. Be honest back.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Tired is a feeling. Finishing is a fact.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

quotes.text: Make peace with the suffering and the suffering makes you fast.
quotes.author: ThreeTri

swim: Exhale continuously underwater through your nose so you only inhale during the rotation, avoiding the panicked breath-hold cycle.
Initiate each stroke by rotating from the hips and core, not by reaching with the shoulder, to engage larger muscles.
Keep your head in a neutral position with eyes looking down and slightly forward to lift your hips and reduce drag.
Bilateral-breathe every three strokes in training to balance your stroke and prepare for sighting on either side.
Practice the catch by pressing water backward with a high elbow rather than pushing down with a straight arm.
Use a pull buoy to isolate and feel your arm catch, but limit reliance so you do not lose kick fitness.
Swim with a tempo trainer beeper to lock in a consistent stroke rate before race day.
Sight open-water style by lifting only your goggles above the surface like a crocodile, then dropping back down to breathe.
Practice sighting every six to ten strokes in the pool by glancing at a fixed point on the wall.
Draft legally behind another swimmer's hip or feet in open water to save up to ten percent of your energy.
Enter the water with your fingertips first and extend forward before catching to maximize your stroke length.
Keep a relaxed, loose hand on recovery rather than a tense fist to conserve forearm energy.
Do catch-up drill to lengthen your stroke and force a complete extension on each side.
Practice deep-water starts and treading so a mass swim start does not spike your heart rate.
Acclimate to cold open water by splashing your face and neck before the gun to blunt the gasp reflex.
Wear your wetsuit on a few training swims to learn how its buoyancy changes your body position and breathing.
Apply anti-chafe lube to your neck, underarms, and ankles before pulling on a wetsuit to prevent raw spots.
Practice removing your wetsuit quickly by peeling it to your waist while running toward transition.
Warm up with easy swimming and a few short race-pace surges before the start to open your stroke.
Pace the swim by effort, not ego, and hold back in the first hundred meters when adrenaline tempts you to sprint.
Do hypoxic breathing sets sparingly to build comfort with breath control, never to the point of dizziness.
Strengthen your kick with short fin sets, but in racing keep a steady two-beat kick to save your legs for the bike.
Roll your body so your shoulders clear the water on recovery, reducing shoulder strain and drag.
Film yourself underwater or have a coach watch to catch a crossover that pulls you off a straight line.
Practice swimming in a group during open-water sessions so contact and bumping feel normal on race day.
Fix a sinking-leg habit by pressing your chest slightly down and engaging your core rather than kicking harder.
Do sets at goal race pace with short rest to teach your body what sustainable speed feels like.
Use a tinted or mirrored goggle lens for bright open water and a clear lens for overcast or dawn starts.
Test and defog your goggles before the start, and carry a spare pair to transition for peace of mind.
Keep your stroke smooth and avoid over-rotating, which can stall your momentum and waste energy.
Strengthen rotator cuff and scapular muscles with resistance bands to prevent swimmer's shoulder.
Increase weekly swim volume gradually, no more than about ten percent, to avoid shoulder overuse injury.
Finish each stroke by pushing past your hip toward your thigh rather than exiting early at your waist.
Repeat a calming mantra like long and strong when the crowd or cold spikes your anxiety mid-swim.
Practice navigating toward a large landmark above the buoys, since small buoys vanish in chop and glare.
Do open-water race-simulation swims to learn how waves and lack of a black line change your rhythm.
Hydrate well before the swim, since you sweat in the water even though you cannot feel it.
Spot the exit and any timing arch during your warm-up so you swim a direct line to the swim-to-bike transition.
Keep your kick narrow and from the hips, letting ankles stay loose and floppy for propulsion.
Build a pre-race routine of breathing and visualization to enter the water calm and focused.
bike: Get a professional bike fit to dial in saddle height and reach, preventing knee pain and improving power.
Maintain a cadence around eighty-five to ninety-five rpm to spare your leg muscles for the run.
Pace the bike by power or perceived effort, holding back early so you do not blow up before the run.
Practice eating and drinking while riding so taking on fuel at speed feels automatic on race day.
Aim for roughly thirty to sixty grams of carbohydrate per hour on the bike for events over an hour.
Drink to thirst plus a planned schedule, targeting roughly one bottle per hour in moderate heat.
Add electrolytes to your bottles in hot conditions to replace sodium lost through sweat.
Practice mounting and dismounting at the transition line with shoes already clipped to the pedals.
Do brick workouts, riding then immediately running, to adapt your legs to the jelly-leg feeling.
Hold an aero position on the flats to cut wind drag, which is your biggest enemy at race speed.
Keep your upper body relaxed and elbows slightly bent to absorb road buzz and reduce fatigue.
Soft-pedal and spin easy for the final few minutes of the bike to prime your legs for running.
Shift to an easier gear before you stop or hit a hill so you can spin up instead of grinding.
Anticipate hills by shifting early and staying seated to maintain steady power without spiking effort.
Practice cornering by looking through the turn, leaning the bike, and keeping your inside pedal up.
Check tire pressure before every ride and carry a spare tube, levers, and CO2 or a pump.
Learn to change a flat quickly at home so a puncture does not end your race.
Ride in varied conditions, including wind and rain, so race-day weather does not rattle you.
Do not coast through the whole descent; pedal where it is fast and recover where it is steep.
Keep your bottles and nutrition in the same reachable spots every ride to build muscle memory.
Fuel early and often rather than waiting until you feel hungry or your energy crashes.
Do interval sessions at threshold to raise the pace you can sustain for an Olympic-distance bike leg.
Include long steady rides to build the aerobic endurance base that underpins all your speed.
Practice race-pace efforts in your aero bars so the position feels strong rather than cramped.
Stretch and mobilize your hip flexors and hamstrings, which tighten from hours in the saddle.
Use chamois cream on long rides to prevent saddle sores and skin breakdown.
Wear sunglasses to protect your eyes from grit, bugs, and wind that can blur your vision.
Lay out your bike gear in transition in the exact order you will use it to avoid fumbling.
Rehearse the full transition flow, helmet on before you touch the bike, to avoid a penalty.
Keep your head up and scan the road ahead for hazards, debris, and other riders.
Stay outside the draft zone in non-drafting races by passing decisively and dropping back legally.
Build leg strength with low-cadence high-resistance intervals to improve hill power.
Do single-leg pedaling drills to smooth out dead spots and develop an even, circular stroke.
Eat a familiar pre-race breakfast a few hours before the start and never try new food on race day.
Keep your gaze and effort steady on long false flats that quietly drain power without feeling steep.
Recover with easy spin rides between hard sessions to flush your legs and aid adaptation.
Replace worn brake pads and tires well before race week so equipment failure is not a worry.
Use a mantra like smooth and steady to hold form when the effort climbs late in the bike leg.
Keep your weight balanced and brake before corners, not in them, to stay upright on wet roads.
Get comfortable riding close to others in legal-draft races by practicing in a group beforehand.
run: Run off the bike in every brick session so your legs learn to find their stride within the first kilometer.
Start the run conservatively, since adrenaline and fresh enthusiasm tempt you to go out far too fast.
Aim for a cadence near one hundred eighty steps per minute to reduce overstriding and impact.
Land with your foot under your hips rather than reaching out in front to lower braking forces.
Keep your posture tall with a slight forward lean from the ankles, not a hunch at the waist.
Relax your shoulders, hands, and jaw, since tension upper body wastes energy you need for your legs.
Breathe rhythmically, syncing breaths to footstrikes, to settle your effort and oxygen delivery.
Practice taking gels or fluids while running so race-day fueling does not upset your stomach.
Sip water or sports drink at aid stations, pinching the cup so you can drink while moving.
Build weekly mileage gradually, following the roughly ten percent rule, to avoid overuse injuries.
Do tempo runs at a comfortably hard pace to raise the speed you can hold for the whole run leg.
Include interval sessions to sharpen your top-end speed and running economy.
Run easy days truly easy so your hard days can be hard and your body recovers between them.
Strengthen your glutes and hips with bridges and clamshells to stabilize your stride and prevent knee pain.
Do strides, short controlled accelerations, after easy runs to maintain leg speed and form.
Rotate your running shoes and replace them roughly every five to seven hundred kilometers.
Choose race shoes you have trained in, never brand-new shoes that could cause blisters.
Use elastic speed laces so you can slip shoes on instantly in transition.
Apply anti-chafe balm to your inner thighs, underarms, and feet before the run to prevent raw skin.
Practice running in the heat to train your body to cool itself and tolerate higher core temperatures.
Pour water over your head and neck at aid stations on hot days to lower your core temperature.
Negative-split the run by holding back early and finishing faster as you settle into rhythm.
Shorten your stride and lean slightly into hills, then open up and let gravity work on the descents.
Foam-roll your calves, quads, and IT band regularly to keep tissue supple and reduce injury risk.
Stretch your hip flexors, which shorten from cycling and can limit your running stride.
Warm up the run muscles with dynamic drills like leg swings and high knees before key sessions.
Practice the transition run-out from the bike, including racking your bike and pulling on your shoes fast.
Set off-the-bike cadence high and stride short for the first minutes to override stiff, sluggish legs.
Use a mantra like light and quick to reset your form when fatigue makes your stride heavy.
Break the run into segments, focusing only on reaching the next aid station or landmark.
Keep your arms swinging forward and back, not across your body, to avoid wasting rotational energy.
Train your gut by consuming your planned race-day carbs during long workouts to avoid GI distress.
Do not start any new supplement, gel, or drink on race day; rehearse all nutrition beforehand.
Include one weekly long run to build the aerobic endurance that supports your race pace.
Practice running on the same surface and terrain as your race so the footing feels familiar.
Listen to early niggles and back off, since most running injuries are overuse problems caught too late.
Cool down with easy jogging after hard runs to gradually lower your heart rate and clear your legs.
Pace by perceived effort late in the race when heat and fatigue make your usual pace feel harder.
Drive your knees and push off your toes on the final stretch to finish strong and upright.
Visualize the finish line and your goal time during training to build mental resilience for the late miles.

legends.name: Jan Frodeno
legends.blurb: The German powerhouse who became the first athlete to win both Olympic triathlon gold (Beijing 2008) and the Ironman World Championship, claiming the Hawaii crown three times and once smashing the long-distance world record.

legends.name: Daniela Ryf
legends.blurb: Nicknamed 'Angry Bird,' the Swiss star has dominated the women's Ironman scene with five world titles and a record-shattering Kona run, complementing five Ironman 70.3 world championships.

legends.name: Alistair Brownlee
legends.blurb: The only triathlete to win back-to-back Olympic individual golds (London 2012, Rio 2016), the elder Brownlee defined an era of British dominance with relentless front-running aggression.

legends.name: Jonathan Brownlee
legends.blurb: The most decorated triathlete in Olympic history with three medals, Jonny capped his career with mixed-relay gold in Tokyo, racing shoulder-to-shoulder with brother Alistair through the 2010s.

legends.name: Chrissie Wellington
legends.blurb: Undefeated across all thirteen of her Ironman-distance races, the four-time world champion famously won the 2011 Kona title just two weeks after a bloody bike crash.

legends.name: Mark Allen
legends.blurb: A six-time Ironman World Champion dubbed by ESPN 'The Greatest Endurance Athlete of All Time,' the American won the legendary 1989 'Iron War' duel with Dave Scott in Kona.

legends.name: Kristian Blummenfelt
legends.blurb: The Norwegian phenom won Olympic gold in Tokyo and an Ironman world title within months, then became the first person ever to complete an Ironman-distance triathlon in under seven hours.

legends.name: Gwen Jorgensen
legends.blurb: A former tax accountant who traded spreadsheets for the podium, Jorgensen won the USA's first Olympic triathlon gold in Rio 2016 after back-to-back World Series titles.

legends.name: Simon Whitfield
legends.blurb: The Canadian who won the very first Olympic triathlon gold in Sydney 2000, picking himself up off the tarmac after a 15-rider bike crash to sprint to victory.

legends.name: Eliud Kipchoge
legends.blurb: The two-time Olympic marathon champion and former world-record holder who, in 2019, became the first human to run 26.2 miles in under two hours with his 1:59:40 in Vienna.

legends.name: Kenenisa Bekele
legends.blurb: The Ethiopian master of the track who long held the 5,000m and 10,000m world records, swept both golds at Beijing 2008, and reigned over cross-country with eleven world titles.

legends.name: Paula Radcliffe
legends.blurb: The British marathon icon whose astonishing 2:15:25 women's world record stood for sixteen years, paired with World Championship and multiple London and New York Marathon victories.

legends.name: Emil Zatopek
legends.blurb: The grimacing 'Czech Locomotive' who pulled off the impossible at Helsinki 1952, winning the 5,000m, 10,000m, and marathon at a single Olympics, a triple never repeated.

legends.name: Haile Gebrselassie
legends.blurb: The endlessly smiling Ethiopian great who set 27 world records, won double Olympic 10,000m gold, and broke the marathon barrier of 2:04 in Berlin.

legends.name: Mo Farah
legends.blurb: Britain's most successful track distance runner, Sir Mo completed the 5,000m/10,000m 'double-double' at London 2012 and Rio 2016 after being trafficked to the UK as a child.

legends.name: Tirunesh Dibaba
legends.blurb: The 'Baby-Faced Destroyer' from Ethiopia who claimed three Olympic golds, five world titles, and was the first woman to win the 5,000m/10,000m double at a single World Championships.

legends.name: Steve Prefontaine
legends.blurb: The charismatic, front-running American icon who held every U.S. record from 2,000m to 10,000m before his death at 24, fueling the 1970s running boom.

legends.name: Sifan Hassan
legends.blurb: The Dutch range-defying star who at Paris 2024 became the first woman to win Olympic gold in the marathon, 5,000m, and 10,000m, after an unprecedented triple-medal haul in Tokyo.

legends.name: Michael Phelps
legends.blurb: The most decorated Olympian of all time with 28 medals, the 'Baltimore Bullet' won a record eight golds at a single Games in Beijing 2008.

legends.name: Katie Ledecky
legends.blurb: The most decorated female swimmer in history, the American distance queen owns the 800m and 1500m freestyle world records and a wall of Olympic and world golds.

legends.name: Ian Thorpe
legends.blurb: The 'Thorpedo,' whose size-17 feet powered him to five Olympic golds and eleven world titles, became Australia's swimming hero at the 2000 Sydney Games.

legends.name: Mark Spitz
legends.blurb: The mustachioed American who won seven golds in seven world-record swims at Munich 1972, a feat that stood unmatched for 36 years until Phelps.

legends.name: Katinka Hosszu
legends.blurb: Hungary's 'Iron Lady,' a triple Olympic champion and nine-time world champion in the punishing individual medley events, famed for her relentless racing schedule.

legends.name: Caeleb Dressel
legends.blurb: The American sprint king who exploded for five golds at the Tokyo Olympics and holds the 100m butterfly world record, regarded as one of the greatest sprinters ever.

legends.name: Adam Peaty
legends.blurb: The dominant British breaststroker who was the first man under 57 seconds in the 100m, defending his Olympic title and rewriting the event's record books.

legends.name: Dawn Fraser
legends.blurb: The Australian trailblazer and one of only a handful of swimmers to win the same individual Olympic event three times, taking 100m freestyle gold in 1956, 1960, and 1964.

legends.name: Ariarne Titmus
legends.blurb: Australia's 'Terminator,' a four-time Olympic champion who dethroned Katie Ledecky in the 400m freestyle and held the 200m freestyle world record.

legends.name: Eddy Merckx
legends.blurb: Simply 'The Cannibal,' the Belgian widely held as the greatest cyclist ever, amassing 525 wins, five Tours de France, and all five Monuments in an 18-year career.

legends.name: Tadej Pogacar
legends.blurb: The Slovenian sensation 'Pogi' who has won four Tours de France and a rare Giro-Tour-Worlds Triple Crown, attacking like a throwback to Merckx himself.

legends.name: Marianne Vos
legends.blurb: The Dutch 'GOAT' of women's cycling, conquering road, track, and cyclo-cross with Olympic golds, three road world titles, and eight cyclo-cross world championships.

legends.name: Bernard Hinault
legends.blurb: The fierce French 'Badger,' a five-time Tour de France winner and the last Frenchman to claim the race, who ruled the peloton as its undisputed patron.

legends.name: Miguel Indurain
legends.blurb: 'Big Mig' of Spain, the only rider to win five consecutive Tours de France (1991-1995), crushing rivals with his extraordinary time-trialing engine.

legends.name: Fabian Cancellara
legends.blurb: Switzerland's 'Spartacus,' a four-time time-trial world champion and double Olympic gold medalist who bullied the cobbled classics with wins at Flanders and Roubaix.

legends.name: Mark Cavendish
legends.blurb: The 'Manx Missile,' the greatest sprinter in cycling history, who broke Eddy Merckx's record with a remarkable 35 Tour de France stage victories.

legends.name: Annemiek van Vleuten
legends.blurb: The Dutch all-rounder who in 2023 became the first woman to win all three Grand Tours, adding multiple world titles and an Olympic gold to a fearless late-blooming career.

legends.name: Jonas Vingegaard
legends.blurb: The Danish climbing genius who rose from a fish-factory job to win the Tour de France twice, anchoring one of cycling's greatest modern rivalries with Pogacar.

day_milestones.title: 90 days out
day_milestones.message: Three months of runway and the base is yours to build — every easy mile you bank now is a deposit you'll cash in on the Mallorca shoreline. Start steady, stay hungry.

day_milestones.title: 75 to go
day_milestones.message: The hard, unglamorous weeks are exactly where races are won. You're laying brick on brick — keep showing up and the fitness will show up too.

day_milestones.title: 60 days
day_milestones.message: Two months out and the engine's coming alive — swims feel smoother, climbs feel shorter. This is momentum, and momentum compounds.

day_milestones.title: 50 left
day_milestones.message: Halfway through the grind from here, and you've already done the work most people only talk about. Trust the build — Palma is getting closer by the stroke and the pedal.

day_milestones.title: Six weeks out
day_milestones.message: Six weeks. This is the meat of it: big bricks, honest open water, long rides that hurt in the best way. Pour it in now — taper will hand it all back.

day_milestones.title: 30 days
day_milestones.message: One month to Mallorca and you've built something real. The hay's nearly in the barn — sharpen, recover, and let belief catch up to the fitness.

day_milestones.title: 3 weeks
day_milestones.message: Twenty-one days out — the work is done, now it's about freshness. Ease back, sleep like it's training, and let your legs remember how good they are.

day_milestones.title: Two weeks
day_milestones.message: Fourteen sleeps to the start line. The taper is not lost fitness, it's loaded spring. Stay loose, stay smart, and start picturing that finish chute.

day_milestones.title: 10 days
day_milestones.message: Single digits are coming. Everything that matters is already in the bank — now protect it. Short, sharp, and confident from here.

day_milestones.title: One week!
day_milestones.message: Seven days. This is the week of trust: nothing you do now makes you fitter, but plenty can keep you fresh. Rest is your last hard workout.

day_milestones.title: 3 days
day_milestones.message: Three sleeps. Bags by the door, bike dialled, goggles packed. The hard part is behind you — what's left is the fun part you earned.

day_milestones.title: 2 days
day_milestones.message: Forty-eight hours. Legs feel weird and twitchy? That's race-ready energy with nowhere to go yet. Hydrate, breathe, and let it build.

day_milestones.title: Tomorrow.
day_milestones.message: Tomorrow you swim, bike and run through everything you've trained for. Lay it all out tonight, then sleep easy — you are so ready for this.

goal_milestones.title: Off the line
goal_milestones.message: Ten percent of the team goal already in the logbook. The journey of a thousand kilometres starts with three friends refusing to skip the first one.

goal_milestones.title: Quarter done
goal_milestones.message: A full quarter of the distance, swum, ridden and run between the three of you. The base is laid — now stack the next one on top.

goal_milestones.title: Building momentum
goal_milestones.message: Forty percent combined and climbing. This is what consistency looks like when three people pull in the same direction.

goal_milestones.title: Halfway there
goal_milestones.message: Halfway! Every metre from here is closer to Palma than to the start. Look back at how far you've come, then turn and chase the rest.

goal_milestones.title: Past the hump
goal_milestones.message: Sixty percent of the team goal is behind you — the hard middle is done and the line is starting to glow on the horizon. Keep grinding together.

goal_milestones.title: Three quarters
goal_milestones.message: Three quarters of the way there as a team. The shape of the finish is real now — close it out the same way you got here, one session at a time.

goal_milestones.title: So close
goal_milestones.message: Ninety percent. You can practically see the Mallorca coastline from here. Don't let off — finish what the three of you started.

goal_milestones.title: Goal smashed!
goal_milestones.message: One hundred percent — the team distance goal is DONE. Thousands of metres of swim, bike and run, conquered shoulder to shoulder. Take a breath and be proud: you built this together.

race_week: Taper week is here — the fitness is locked in, now guard it like treasure.
Feeling restless and fresh-legged? Good. That's a body begging to race.
You don't need one more hard session. You need sleep, food, and belief.
Trust the training. Every early alarm and brutal brick led to this week.
Light legs, clear mind, full heart — this is exactly how it's supposed to feel.
Nothing left to prove in training. Save the magic for the start line.
Three of you started this. This week, you remind each other why.

race_day.title: This is it — Mallorca. Go get it.
race_day.message: Today the alarm means something different. Ebi, Sia, Alborz — every dark morning swim, every long ride into a headwind, every run when you'd rather have stopped, it all funnels into this single day on the Mallorca coast. You didn't just train for a triathlon, you trained alongside two friends who refused to let you quit. So toe that line together, swim brave, ride bold, run with everything you've got, and chase each other all the way down the finish chute. Whatever the clock says, you already won the part that matters. Now go and make it official. Three friends. Three sports. One unforgettable finish line. GO!

athlete_nudges: Today's easy run is a gift — take it. Easy means easy.
Lace up before your brain files a complaint. The first ten minutes do the convincing.
Swim drills feel boring until the day they make you fast. Today's that investment.
Spin the legs out today. Recovery is a session, not a day off.
One length at a time. The pool doesn't care how the last one felt.
Headwind on the bike? That's free strength training in disguise.
Nail your fuelling today — bonking is a lesson you only need to learn once.
Slow is smooth and smooth is fast. Let today be smooth.
Show up for the warm-up and the workout will take care of itself.
Your zone 2 ride is building the engine everyone else skips. Stay patient.
Breathe bilaterally today, even when it feels awkward — your race self will thank you.
Hills are just the road asking how badly you want it. Answer kindly but firmly.
Rest day? Actually rest. Scrolling training plans is not recovery.
Get in the water even if you're cold. You've never once regretted the swim.
Negative-split this run — start boring, finish proud.
Cadence over force today. Let your legs spin light and quick.
Hydrate before you're thirsty; you're already behind if you wait.
The long ride is long for a reason. Settle in and enjoy the company of the road.
Form first, pace second. Tired running with good form still counts.
Sight the buoy, trust the line, stop second-guessing your stroke.
Today's brick is the secret sauce — run off the bike and remember the feeling.
Five more minutes of mobility tonight saves you five missed sessions later.
Threshold work hurts on purpose. Lean into the discomfort, it's temporary.
Don't chase yesterday's numbers — chase today's effort.
Open water is messy and that's the point. Get comfortable being uncomfortable.
Easy spin, good podcast, sunshine if you can find it. Today's ride is meant to feel good.
Sleep is the most underrated workout you'll do all week. Get to bed.
Stride out the last 100m of every easy run — wake the fast-twitch up.
Eat enough today. You can't build fitness on an empty tank.
Cold, dark, and not feeling it? Future-you on race day is begging present-you to go.
Swim tall, swim long, glide between strokes — chase distance per stroke today.
Climb seated when you can; save the standing for when it really counts.
Trust your taper. Fresh legs feel strange because they're saving themselves.
Celebrate the boring session. Consistency is the whole game.
Run by feel today and leave the watch out of the conversation.
Stretch the hip flexors — the bike tightens them and the run pays the bill.
Strong finish, every time. Teach your body that the end is where you push.
Drills before the main set. Boring now, smooth forever.
You don't have to be fast today. You just have to be there.
Look how far you've come. Now go add one more brick to it.
